text: Trie Pattern: Basic trie structure and operations. Trie (prefix tree) stores strings with shared prefixes. Each node has children[26] and is_end flag. Insert: for each char, create child if not exists, traverse, set is_end. Search: traverse chars, return is_end at last char. StartsWith: traverse chars, return True if all chars exist. Time: O(L) per operation where L=string length.

Trie Pattern: Implementation. class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. class Trie: def insert(self, word): node=self.root; for c in word: if c not in node.children: node.children[c]=TrieNode(); node=node.children[c]; node.is_end=True. Use dict for children (sparse), or array[26] (dense lowercase alphabet). Space: O(ALPHABET * N * L).

Trie Pattern: Word search II (find all words in grid). Build trie from word list. DFS from each cell matching characters against trie. When is_end is True, found a word. Prune DFS when no trie node for current char. Remove found words from trie to avoid duplicates. Time: O(m*n*4^L) but much faster in practice with trie pruning vs searching each word separately.

Trie Pattern: Autocomplete / search suggestions. Insert all words into trie. For a prefix query: navigate to prefix node. DFS from there to collect all words (is_end nodes). Return top-k suggestions. For top-k: store sorted list at each node (space-heavy but O(1) retrieval). For frequency-based: store (frequency, word) at each node. Time: O(L + output_size) per query.

Trie Pattern: Maximum XOR of two numbers (binary trie). Build binary trie from all numbers (MSB to LSB). For each number, query trie to find number giving max XOR: at each bit, prefer opposite bit if exists. Track max. Time: O(32*n) for build and query. Alternative to O(n²) brute force. For max XOR subarray: combine with prefix XOR.

Trie Pattern: Count words with given prefix. Augment TrieNode with count = number of words passing through this node. Increment count on each insert. Prefix count query: navigate to prefix node, return node.count. Time: O(L) per query. Also: count words ending exactly at node with is_end count for exact match counting.

Trie Pattern: Longest word in dictionary built one character at a time. Find longest word where all prefixes also exist. Build trie. BFS from root: only traverse to children where is_end is True (meaning prefix word exists). Longest path reached = answer. Time: O(total_chars). Alternative: sort words, use set to check if all prefixes present.

Trie Pattern: Replace words (word substitution). Given dictionary of root words, replace words in sentence with shortest matching root. Build trie from roots. For each word in sentence: traverse trie; return root when is_end reached (shortest prefix). If no root found, keep original word. Time: O(dict_chars + sentence_chars). Efficient vs naive O(sentence * dict) approach.

Trie Pattern: Design search autocomplete system. Trie with each node storing list of top-3 sentences (sorted by frequency desc, then lexicographic). Insert and update frequencies. Query: return top-3 at prefix node. Trade-off: expensive insert/update (update all ancestor nodes) but fast query. Alternative: lazy computation at query time with DFS. Time: O(L*3) query, O(L*3) insert.

Trie Pattern: Map sum pairs. Trie where each terminal node stores value. insert(key, val). sum(prefix): return sum of values of all keys starting with prefix. Augment each node with prefix_sum = sum of values in subtree. Update all ancestors on insert. Time: O(L) per operation. Space: O(total_chars). Elegant use of trie for aggregate queries.

Trie Pattern: Wildcard pattern matching in trie. '.' matches any single character. When querying, at '.' node: recurse into ALL children. DFS through trie with wildcard expansion. Is slower than exact match: O(ALPHABET * L) per wildcard. For '*' (match any sequence): more complex backtracking. Use for: add and search words with wildcards, spell checker with substitutions.

Trie Pattern: Compressed trie (Patricia trie / radix tree). Instead of one character per edge, store entire string segments. Reduces node count from O(total_chars) to O(num_words). Each edge labeled with string segment. On insert: find split point, split existing edge. Space: O(num_words) nodes vs O(total_chars) for standard trie. Used in: routing tables (IP lookup), suffix trees.

Trie Pattern: Suffix trie and suffix array. Suffix trie: insert all suffixes of a string. Allows pattern search in O(pattern_length). Suffix array: sorted array of all suffix starting indices. Build with O(n log n) or O(n) algorithms. LCP (longest common prefix) array enables efficient string operations. Use for: string search, longest repeated substring, number of distinct substrings.

Trie Pattern: Persistent trie. Maintain multiple versions of trie without full copy. On update: create new nodes only along modified path (path copying). Each version shares unchanged structure with previous versions. Space: O(N * L) for N updates on strings of length L. Use for: offline queries on versioned data, range XOR maximum queries.

Trie Pattern: Trie-based sorting (lexicographic). Insert all strings into trie. DFS in alphabetical order (process children in sorted order) to output strings in lexicographic order. Time: O(total_chars) for insert + O(total_chars) for DFS. More efficient than comparison sort O(n * L * log n) when many strings share prefixes. Also used for: alien dictionary ordering.

Trie Pattern: Delete operation in trie. Navigate to end of word. Mark is_end=False. Clean up nodes with no children from bottom up (post-order). A node can be deleted if: not is_end and has no children. Requires parent reference or recursive approach. Time: O(L). Alternative: lazy deletion (don't clean up, just unmark is_end) is simpler but uses more memory.

Trie Pattern: Aho-Corasick algorithm (multi-pattern search). Build trie from all patterns. Compute failure links (like KMP failure function but on trie). Traverse text once, following trie edges and failure links on mismatch. Find all pattern occurrences simultaneously. Time: O(text_length + total_pattern_length + matches). Use for: grep, virus scanners, network intrusion detection.

Trie Pattern: Trie vs hash set for prefix queries. Hash set: O(L) lookup for exact match, O(L*prefix_len) for all-prefix check. Trie: O(L) for exact match, O(L) for prefix check. Use trie when: frequent prefix queries, autocomplete, multiple related lookups sharing prefixes. Use hash set when: only exact matching needed. Trade-off: trie uses more memory but enables efficient prefix operations.

Trie Pattern: Number of distinct substrings. Build suffix automaton or suffix trie. Each path in suffix trie is a unique substring. Count all root-to-node paths. Suffix automaton: more space-efficient (O(n) states). For each state in automaton: add (len[state] - len[link[state]]) to answer. Time: O(n) build, O(n) count. Alternative: suffix array with LCP array: O(n log n).

Trie Pattern: Implement trie with array vs hashmap. Array children[26]: fast access O(1), uses 26 pointers per node regardless of actual children (wastes memory for sparse tries). HashMap children: dynamic size, O(1) average but higher constant, good for large alphabets (Unicode). For competitive programming: array is faster. For production with arbitrary characters: use hashmap or compressed trie.
